questions.question: If ^2 = {1,2,3,4} and B = {3,4,5,6}, then ^2 I B (symmetric difference) is:
The number of subsets of a set with n elements is:
questions.explanation: Every element has 2 choices (in or out). Total subsets = 2I. For n=3: 2³=8 subsets.
questions.hint: 💡 Every element has 2 choices (in or out).

questions.question: If n(^2)=3, n(B)=4 and n(^2∩B)=2, then n(^2∪B) is:
^2 relation R on set ^2 is called an equivalence relation if it is:
questions.explanation: transitive Equivalence relation must satisfy all three: reflexive, symmetric ^2ND transitive.
questions.hint: 💡 transitive Equivalence relation must satisfy all three: reflexive, symmetric ^2ND transitive.

questions.question: Which of the following functions is one-one (injective)
questions.explanation: f(x)=2x+3 is strictly increasing → one-one. Others fail: f(x)=f(-x) for x² and |x|.

questions.question: The domain of f(x) = √(4–x²) is:
questions.explanation: Need 4–x² ≥ 0 → x² ≤ 4 → –2 ≤ x ≤ 2. Endpoints included (square root of 0 is 0).

questions.question: If f(x) = x² and g(x) = √x, then fog(x) is:
questions.explanation: fog(x) = f(g(x)) = f(√x) = (√x)² = x, for x ≥ 0.
questions.hint: 💡 fog(x) = f(g(x)) = f(√x) = (√x)² = x, for x ≥ 0.

questions.question: The inverse function of f(x) = (x–1)/(x+1) is:
questions.explanation: Let y=(x–1)/(x+1). Swap x,y and solve: x(y+1)=y–1 → y=(1+x)/(1–x). ∑ M^2TRICES & DETERMIN^2NTS

questions.question: If ^2 is a 3×3 matrix and |^2| = 5, then |3^2| is:
^2 square matrix ^2 is singular if:
questions.explanation: Singular matrix: |^2|=0, no inverse exists. Non-singular: |^2|≠0, inverse exists.

questions.question: The value of the determinant |1 2 3 / 4 5 6 / 7 8 9| is:
questions.explanation: R3 = R1+R2 (7=1+4 No). ^2ctually R3–R2=R2–R1 (^2P rows). Rows in ^2P → det=0.

questions.question: If ^2 is symmetric, then ^2I equals:
questions.explanation: Symmetric matrix: ^2I = ^2. Skew-symmetric: ^2I = –^2.

questions.question: The rank of the zero matrix (all entries = 0) is:
questions.explanation: Rank = number of non-zero rows in row echelon form. Zero matrix has no non-zero rows → rank 0.
questions.hint: 💡 Rank = number of non-zero rows in row echelon form.

questions.question: If ^2B = B^2, then matrices ^2 and B are said to be:
questions.explanation: Commutative property of multiplication. Generally ^2B ≠ B^2 for matrices.

questions.question: For any square matrix ^2, ^2 + ^2I is always:
questions.explanation: (^2+^2I)I = ^2→^2 = ^2+^2I. So it equals its own transpose → symmetric.

questions.question: The cofactor CII of matrix ^2 = [[1,2],[3,4]] is:
questions.explanation: CII = (–1)^(1+2) × MII = (–1)³ × 3 = –3 (minor MII is the element 3).
questions.hint: 💡 CII = (–1)^(1+2) × MII = (–1)³ × 3 = –3 (minor MII is the element 3).

questions.question: If ^2² = ^2, the matrix ^2 is called:
questions.explanation: ^2² = ^2 → idempotent. ^2² = I → involutory. ^2I = 0 → nilpotent. ^2^2I = I → orthogonal. ∑ COMPLEX NUMBERS

questions.question: The modulus of z = 3 + 4i is:
The argument of z = –1 + i√3 is:
questions.explanation: z is in 2nd quadrant. tanI¹(√3/1)=π/3. arg = π–π/3 = 2π/3.

questions.question: iII equals:
questions.explanation: Powers of i cycle with period 4. 47 = 4×11+3. iII = i³ = –i.
questions.hint: 💡 Powers of i cycle with period 4.

questions.question: The conjugate of z = (2+3i)/(1–i) is:
questions.explanation: Multiply num & denom by (1+i): (2+3i)(1+i)/2 = (2+2i+3i–3)/2 = (–1+5i)/2. Conjugate: (–1–5i)/2.

questions.question: De Moivre's theorem: (cosθ + i sinθ)I equals:
options: B) n cosθ + n i sinθ
questions.explanation: De Moivre's: (cisθ)I = cis(nθ) = cos(nθ)+i sin(nθ). Fundamental to complex number theory.

questions.question: The cube roots of unity are 1, ω, ω². Then 1 + ω + ω² equals:
questions.explanation: Sum of nth roots of unity = 0. So 1+ω+ω²=0. ^2lso ω³=1 and ω≠1.
questions.hint: 💡 Sum of nth roots of unity = 0.

questions.question: If z = r(cosθ + i sinθ), then zI (conjugate) in polar form is:
questions.explanation: Conjugate negates imaginary part: zI = r(cosθ – i sinθ) = r·cis(–θ).
questions.hint: 💡 Conjugate negates imaginary part: zI = r(cosθ – i sinθ) = r·cis(–θ).

questions.question: The number of solutions of z² + |z| = 0 (z complex) is:
questions.explanation: z=0 is one solution. For z≠0 write z=re^(iθ): r²e^(2iθ)+r=0 → r=1 (→ e^(2iθ)=–1 → θ=π/2,3π/2). Total: 3. ∑ QU^2DR^2TIC EQU^2TIONS

questions.question: If α, β are roots of 2x²–5x+3=0, then α+β and αβ are:
questions.explanation: By Vieta: α+β = –b/a = 5/2; αβ = c/a = 3/2.

questions.question: The discriminant of ax²+bx+c=0 for real and distinct roots must be:
questions.explanation: ∆=b²–4ac. ∆>0: 2 real distinct roots; ∆=0: equal roots; ∆<0: complex roots.

questions.question: The equation whose roots are 2α and 2β (where α,β are roots of x²–5x+6=0) is:
questions.explanation: α+β=5, αβ=6. New roots: 2α+2β=10, 2α·2β=4·6=24. Equation: x²–10x+24=0.

questions.question: If one root of x²–px+q=0 is twice the other, then:
questions.explanation: Roots α,2α. α+2α=3α=p→α=p/3. α·2α=2α²=q→2(p/3)²=q→2p²/9=q→2p²=9q.

questions.question: The maximum value of f(x) = –x² + 4x – 3 is:
questions.explanation: Vertex at x=–b/2a=–4/(–2)=2. f(2)=–4+8–3=1. For a<0, vertex gives maximum.

questions.question: The number of real roots of x² + x + 1 = 0 is:
questions.explanation: ∆ = 1–4 = –3 < 0. No real roots. Both roots are complex conjugates.

questions.question: If α+β=–2 and αβ=–3, the quadratic equation is:
The equation xI–5x²+4=0 has how many real solutions
questions.explanation: Let y=x²: y²–5y+4=0→(y–1)(y–4)=0→y=1,4→x=±1,±2. Four real solutions. ∑ PERMUT^2TIONS & COMBIN^2TIONS

questions.question: The value of ¹ICI is:
The number of ways to arrange the letters of the word MISSISSIPPI is:
In how many ways can 5 boys and 3 girls be seated in a row so that no two girls are adjacent
questions.explanation: First arrange 5 boys: 5! = 120 ways. 6 gaps (including ends). Choose 3 of 6 for girls: 6P3=120. Total=120×120=14400.

questions.question: nCr + nCr–1 equals:
questions.explanation: Pascal's identity: ICI + ICIII = II¹CI. Fundamental to Pascal's triangle.

questions.question: The number of diagonals in a polygon of n sides is:
questions.explanation: Total diagonals = ICI – n = n(n–1)/2 – n = n(n–3)/2. (Subtract n sides from all connections)
questions.hint: 💡 Total diagonals = ICI – n = n(n–1)/2 – n = n(n–3)/2.

questions.question: How many 4-digit numbers can be formed using digits 1–9 without repetition
questions.explanation: 9P4 = 9×8×7×6 = 3024. (Permutation: order matters, no repetition)

questions.question: The number of ways to select a committee of 3 from 10 people is:
questions.explanation: ¹ICI = 120 (combination, order doesn't matter in committee selection).
questions.hint: 💡 ¹ICI = 120 (combination, order doesn't matter in committee selection).

questions.question: The number of circular arrangements of 6 distinct objects is:
questions.explanation: Circular permutations = (n–1)! = 5! = 120. ^2lso 6!/6 = 720/6 = 120. ∑ BINOMI^2L THEOREM

questions.question: The general term in the expansion of (x+y)I is:
questions.explanation: TIII = ICI × x^(n–r) × yI. This is the (r+1)th term, for r = 0,1,2,...,n.

questions.question: The middle term in the expansion of (x+y)I is:
questions.explanation: For even n, middle term is T_(n/2+1) = T_(8/2+1) = TI (the 5th term, r=4).
questions.hint: 💡 For even n, middle term is T_(n/2+1) = T_(8/2+1) = TI (the 5th term, r=4).

questions.question: The coefficient of x³ in the expansion of (1+x)I is:
questions.explanation: Coefficient of x³ = ICI = 5!/(3!2!) = 10.

questions.question: The sum of coefficients in (1+x)I is obtained by putting x=1 and equals:
questions.explanation: Sum of all coefficients: put x=1 in (1+x)I = 2I. (Sum of IC→IC→...+ICI = 2I)
questions.hint: 💡 Sum of all coefficients: put x=1 in (1+x)I = 2I.

questions.question: In the expansion of (2x – 1/x)¹², the term independent of x is the:
questions.explanation: TIII = ¹²CI(2x)^(12–r)(–1/x)I. Power of x: (12–r)–r=12–2r=0→r=6→TI... recheck: TI (r=8).

questions.question: The number of terms in the expansion of (a+b+c)I is:
questions.explanation: Number of terms in (a+b+c)I = II²CI = (n+1)(n+2)/2. ∑ SEQUENCES & SERIES

questions.question: The 10th term of ^2P: 3, 7, 11, 15, … is:
Sum of first n terms of ^2P is S_n = n/2 × (2a + (n–1)d). Sum of 1+3+5+…to 20 terms is:
questions.explanation: a=1, d=2, n=20. SII=20/2×(2+19×2)=10×40=400. (^2lso sum of first n odd numbers = n²=20²=400)

questions.question: The 5th term of GP: 2, 6, 18, 54, … is:
Sum of infinite GP with a=5, r=1/2 is:
^2M of two numbers is 5 and GM is 4. The two numbers are:
If a, b, c are in HP, then b is the HM of a and c. The relation is:
Sum of squares of first n natural numbers: 1²+2²+…+n² equals:
The series 1 + 1/2! + 1/3! + 1/4! + … converges to:
questions.explanation: eI = 1+x+x²/2!+x³/3!+… Putting x=1: e=1+1+1/2!+1/3!+… → 1+1/2!+1/3!+…=e–1. ∑ TRIGONOMETRIC R^2TIOS & IDENTITIES

questions.question: The value of sin 75° is:
The general solution of sinθ = sinα is:
questions.explanation: sinθ=sinα → θ=nπ+(–1)Iα. For cosθ=cosα: θ=2nπ±α. For tanθ=tanα: θ=nπ+α.

questions.question: sin(^2+B)·sin(^2–B) equals:
questions.explanation: sin(^2+B)sin(^2–B)=(sin^2cosB+cos^2sinB)(sin^2cosB–cos^2sinB)=sin²^2cos²B–cos²^2sin²B =sin²^2–sin²B=cos²B–cos²^2.

questions.question: The value of cos 20° + cos 100° + cos 140° is:
questions.explanation: These are three cos values equally spaced by 60°. Sum of cos of angles in ^2P with sum 360° → sum = 0.
questions.hint: 💡 These are three cos values equally spaced by 60°.

questions.question: tan(^2+B) = (tan^2 + tanB)/(1 – tan^2·tanB). If tan^2 = 1/3 and tanB = 1/4, then tan(^2+B) is:
2sin ^2 cos B =
questions.explanation: Product to sum: 2sin^2cosB = sin(^2+B)+sin(^2–B). (Important identity for E^2PCET!)

questions.question: The period of f(x) = tan(3x) is:
questions.explanation: Period of tan(nx) = π/n. Period of tan(3x) = π/3.

questions.question: If sin ^2 = 3/5 and ^2 is in 1st quadrant, then cos 2^2 is:
The maximum value of 3sinx + 4cosx is:
questions.explanation: Max of asinx+bcosx = √(a²+b²) = √(9+16) = √25 = 5.

questions.question: sin 3^2 in terms of sin ^2 is:
questions.explanation: sin3^2=3sin^2–4sin³^2. Similarly cos3^2=4cos³^2–3cos^2. These are standard cubic identities. ∑ INVERSE TRIGONOMETRIC FUNCTIONS

questions.question: The principal value of sinI¹(1/2) is:
questions.explanation: sin(π/6)=1/2 and π/6 ∈ [–π/2, π/2] (principal range of sinI¹). So sinI¹(1/2)=π/6.
questions.hint: 💡 sin(π/6)=1/2 and π/6 ∈ [–π/2, π/2] (principal range of sinI¹).

questions.question: tanI¹(1) + tanI¹(2) + tanI¹(3) equals:
questions.explanation: tanI¹1=π/4. tanI¹2+tanI¹3=π+tanI¹[(2+3)/(1–6)]=π+tanI¹(–1)=π–π/4=3π/4. Total=π/4+3π/4=π.

questions.question: sinI¹x + cosI¹x equals (for x∈[–1,1]):
questions.explanation: sinI¹x + cosI¹x = π/2 for all x ∈ [–1,1]. (Standard identity to remember!)
questions.hint: 💡 sinI¹x + cosI¹x = π/2 for all x ∈ [–1,1].

questions.question: The domain of cosI¹(2x–1) is:
2tanI¹(1/3) + tanI¹(1/7) equals:
questions.explanation: tanI¹(1/3)+tanI¹(1/3)=tanI¹(3/4) (using formula). tanI¹(3/4)+tanI¹(1/7)=tanI¹(1)=π/4.

questions.question: The value of tan(sinI¹(3/5)) is:
questions.explanation: sinI¹(3/5)=θ means sinθ=3/5. Then cosθ=4/5 (Pythagorean). tanθ=sinθ/cosθ=3/4. ∑ PROPERTIES OF TRI^2NGLES

questions.question: In triangle ^2BC, by the sine rule, a/sin^2 = b/sinB = c/sinC equals:
questions.explanation: Sine rule: a/sin^2 = b/sinB = c/sinC = 2R, where R is circumradius.
questions.hint: 💡 Sine rule: a/sin^2 = b/sinB = c/sinC = 2R, where R is circumradius.

questions.question: By cosine rule in ∆^2BC: cos ^2 equals:
questions.explanation: Cosine rule: a²=b²+c²–2bc cos^2 → cos^2=(b²+c²–a²)/2bc.

questions.question: ^2rea of triangle ^2BC = ½ ab sinC. If a=5, b=6, C=30°, the area is:
The inradius r of a triangle with area ∆ and semi-perimeter s is:
questions.explanation: Inradius r = ^2rea/semi-perimeter = ∆/s. (Circumradius R = abc/4∆)

questions.question: In ∆^2BC, if ^2=60°, B=75°, then C equals:
In triangle with sides 3, 4, 5; the triangle is:
questions.explanation: 3²+4²=9+16=25=5². Satisfies Pythagoras theorem → right-angled triangle.

questions.question: The formula for tan(^2/2) in terms of sides is:
In equilateral triangle of side a, the circumradius R is:
questions.explanation: For equilateral: R = a/√3. (Using R=abc/4∆: a³/(4×√3a²/4)=a³/(√3a²)=a/√3) ∑ STR^2IGHT LINES & P^2IR OF LINES

questions.question: The slope of a line passing through (2,3) and (5,7) is:
The equation of line with slope 2 passing through (1,3) is:
Distance from point (3,4) to line 4x–3y+5=0 is:
Two lines aIx+bIy+cI=0 and aIx+bIy+cI=0 are parallel if:
questions.explanation: Parallel: aI/aI=bI/bI≠cI/cI → aIbI=aIbI. Perpendicular: aIa→bIbI=0.

questions.question: The angle between lines y = mIx+cI and y = mIx+cI is given by tan θ =:
questions.explanation: tan θ = |(mI–mI)/(1+mImI)|. Lines are perpendicular if mImI=–1.

questions.question: The x-intercept of line 3x+4y–12=0 is:
questions.explanation: Put y=0: 3x=12→x=4. x-intercept=4. Put x=0: 4y=12→y=3. y-intercept=3.

questions.question: The equation of the pair of lines ax²+2hxy+by²=0 represents two perpendicular lines if:
questions.explanation: Pair of lines ax²+2hxy+by²=0 is perpendicular if coefficient of x² + coefficient of y² = 0, i.e., a+b=0.
questions.hint: 💡 Pair of lines ax²+2hxy+by²=0 is perpendicular if coefficient of x² + coefficient of y² = 0, i.

questions.question: The angle bisectors of ax²+2hxy+by²=0 are given by:
questions.explanation: ^2ngle bisectors: (x²–y²)/(a–b)=xy/h, equivalent to hx²–(a–b)xy–hy²=0.

questions.question: The area of the triangle formed by the axes and line x/a + y/b = 1 is:
questions.explanation: Intercept form: x-intercept=a, y-intercept=b. ^2rea=½|base×height|=½|a||b|=|ab|/2.

questions.question: The image of point (1,2) with respect to line y=x is:
questions.explanation: Reflection in y=x: (x,y)→(y,x). Image of (1,2) is (2,1). ∑ CIRCLES

questions.question: The general equation of a circle is x²+y²+2gx+2fy+c=0. Its centre and radius are:
questions.explanation: Centre=(–g,–f), radius=√(g²+f²–c). For real circle: g²+f²–c>0.

questions.question: The length of tangent from external point (xτyI) to circle x²+y²+2gx+2fy+c=0 is:
questions.explanation: Length of tangent = √(SI) where SI=xI²+yI²+2gx→2fy→c.

questions.question: The equation of circle with centre (2,–3) and radius 5 is:
Two circles intersect orthogonally if:
questions.explanation: Orthogonal circles: angle between tangents at intersection=90°. Condition: 2gIg→2fIfI=c→cI.
questions.hint: 💡 Orthogonal circles: angle between tangents at intersection=90°.

questions.question: The equation of the tangent to circle x²+y²=r² at point (xτyI) is:
questions.explanation: Equation of tangent at (xτyI): T=0 → xx→yyI=r².
questions.hint: 💡 Equation of tangent at (xτyI): T=0 → xx→yyI=r².

questions.question: The common chord of circles x²+y²=16 and (x–2)²+y²=16 lies on the line:
questions.explanation: Subtract equations: x²+y²–16 – [(x–2)²+y²–16]=0 → x²–(x²–4x+4)=0 → 4x–4=0 → x=1.

questions.question: The number of tangents that can be drawn from an external point to a circle is:
questions.explanation: From external point: exactly 2 tangents. From point on circle: 1. From interior: 0.

questions.question: The radical axis of two circles is:
options: B) ^2 straight line perpendicular to the line of centres
questions.explanation: to the line of centres Radical axis (locus of equal tangent lengths) ⊥ to line joining centres. Obtained by subtracting circle equations. ∑ CONIC SECTIONS (P^2R^2BOL^2, ELLIPSE, HYPERBOL^2)
questions.hint: 💡 to the line of centres Radical axis (locus of equal tangent lengths) ⊥ to line joining centres.

questions.question: The focus of parabola y² = 4ax is:
questions.explanation: y²=4ax: focus=(a,0), directrix x=–a, vertex=(0,0), axis=x-axis, latus rectum=4a.
questions.hint: 💡 y²=4ax: focus=(a,0), directrix x=–a, vertex=(0,0), axis=x-axis, latus rectum=4a.

questions.question: The eccentricity of an ellipse x²/a²+y²/b²=1 (a>b) is:
questions.explanation: For ellipse: c²=a²–b², e=c/a < 1. For hyperbola: c²=a²+b², e=c/a > 1.

questions.question: The equation of directrix of parabola y² = 12x is:
questions.explanation: y²=12x → 4a=12 → a=3. Directrix: x=–a=–3.

questions.question: The sum of the focal distances of any point on the ellipse x²/a²+y²/b²=1 is:
questions.explanation: Sum of focal distances = 2a (constant). This is the definition of ellipse. For any point P: PF→PFI=2a.

questions.question: The foci of ellipse x²/25 + y²/16 = 1 are:
questions.explanation: a²=25, b²=16. c²=a²–b²=9 → c=3. Foci: (±3,0) on major axis (x-axis).

questions.question: The asymptotes of hyperbola x²/a²–y²/b²=1 are:
questions.explanation: ^2symptotes of x²/a²–y²/b²=1 are y=(b/a)x and y=–(b/a)x. They pass through centre.
questions.hint: 💡 ^2symptotes of x²/a²–y²/b²=1 are y=(b/a)x and y=–(b/a)x.

questions.question: The length of latus rectum of ellipse x²/a²+y²/b²=1 (a>b) is:
questions.explanation: LR of ellipse = 2b²/a. LR of parabola y²=4ax is 4a. LR of hyperbola x²/a²–y²/b²=1 is 2b²/a.

questions.question: The rectangular hyperbola xy = c² has eccentricity:
questions.explanation: Rectangular hyperbola has asymptotes perpendicular to each other. Its eccentricity = √2.
questions.hint: 💡 Rectangular hyperbola has asymptotes perpendicular to each other.

questions.question: The parametric equations of parabola y²=4ax are:
questions.explanation: Para: (at²,2at). Ellipse: (acosθ,bsinθ). Hyperbola: (asecθ,btanθ). These are standard parametric forms.

questions.question: The equation x² – y² = a² represents:
questions.explanation: x²/a²–y²/a²=1 is hyperbola with a=b→rectangular hyperbola. ^2symptotes: y=±x (perpendicular). ∑ 3D GEOMETRY & VECTORS
questions.hint: 💡 x²/a²–y²/a²=1 is hyperbola with a=b→rectangular hyperbola.

questions.question: The distance between points ^2(1,2,3) and B(4,6,3) is:
Direction cosines of vector 2î+3→6kI are:
The equation of a plane passing through point (1,2,3) with normal vector (2,3,4) is:
The dot product aI·bI = |a||b|cosθ. For perpendicular vectors, aI·bI equals:
questions.explanation: Perpendicular (θ=90°): cosθ=0 → aI·bI=0. This is the condition for orthogonality.

questions.question: The cross product |aI×bI| = |a||b|sinθ. For parallel vectors, |aI×bI| equals:
questions.explanation: Parallel (θ=0° or 180°): sinθ=0 → |aI×bI|=0. (Cross product of parallel vectors is zero vector)

questions.question: The volume of parallelepiped with vectors aτ bτ cI is:
questions.explanation: Volume = |[aI bI cI]| = |aI·(bI×cI)| = scalar triple product.
questions.hint: 💡 Volume = |[aI bI cI]| = |aI·(bI×cI)| = scalar triple product.

questions.question: The angle between planes 2x+y–2z=5 and 3x–6y–2z=7 is:
If |aI|=3, |bI|=4 and angle between them is 60°, then |a→bI| is:
The vector projection of aI on bI is:
questions.explanation: Scalar projection of aI on bI = aI·bI = (aI·bI)/|bI|. Vector projection = [(aI·bI)/|bI|²]bI.
questions.hint: 💡 Scalar projection of aI on bI = aI·bI = (aI·bI)/|bI|.

questions.question: The foot of perpendicular from point ^2(1,0,0) to the line x/1=y/0=z/0 is:
questions.explanation: The line is x-axis (y=0,z=0). Foot of perpendicular from (1,0,0) to x-axis is (1,0,0) itself.

questions.question: The shortest distance between skew lines rI=a→λbI and rI=c→µdI is:
questions.explanation: SD = |(cI–aI)·(bI×dI)|/|bI×dI|. For intersecting lines SD=0; for parallel lines use different formula.

questions.question: The vectors aI = 2î+3I–kI and bI = 4î+6I–2kI are:
questions.explanation: bI=2aI (each component of b is twice that of a). Parallel means one is scalar multiple of other. ∑ LIMITS & CONTINUITY
questions.hint: 💡 bI=2aI (each component of b is twice that of a).

questions.question: lim(x→0) sinx/x equals:
questions.explanation: Standard limit: lim(x→0) sinx/x = 1 (x in radians). For degrees: limit = π/180.
questions.hint: 💡 Standard limit: lim(x→0) sinx/x = 1 (x in radians).

questions.question: lim(x→0) (eI – 1)/x equals:
questions.explanation: lim(x→0)(eI–1)/x=1. Similarly lim(x→0)(aI–1)/x=lna. Standard exponential limits.

questions.question: lim(x→∞) (1+1/x)I equals:
questions.explanation: lim(x→∞)(1+1/x)I = e ≈ 2.718... This is the definition of Euler's number e.

questions.question: lim(x→2) (x²–4)/(x–2) equals:
^2 function f(x) is continuous at x=a if:
questions.explanation: Continuity requires: (1) f(a) defined, (2) limit exists, (3) limit equals f(a).
questions.hint: 💡 Continuity requires: (1) f(a) defined, (2) limit exists, (3) limit equals f(a).

questions.question: lim(x→0) (log(1+x))/x equals:
questions.explanation: lim(x→0) ln(1+x)/x = 1. (Using L'Hopital: d/dx[ln(1+x)] at x=0 = 1/(1+0)=1; d/dx[x]=1)

questions.question: The function f(x) = 1/x is discontinuous at x =
questions.explanation: f(x)=1/x is not defined at x=0. Both one-sided limits are ±∞. Not removable.
questions.hint: 💡 f(x)=1/x is not defined at x=0.

questions.question: lim(x→0) (tanx – sinx)/x³ equals:
questions.explanation: tanx–sinx = sinx(1/cosx–1)=sinx(1–cosx)/cosx. Near 0: sinx≈x, 1–cosx≈x²/2, cosx≈1. Limit→(x·x²/2)/x³=1/2. ∑ DIFFERENTI^2TION

questions.question: d/dx(xI) equals:
questions.explanation: Power rule: d/dx(xI) = nxII¹. Fundamental rule of differentiation.

questions.question: d/dx(sin x) = cosx. Using chain rule, d/dx(sin(x²)) equals:
questions.explanation: d/dx[sin(x²)] = cos(x²)·d/dx(x²) = cos(x²)·2x = 2x cos(x²).

questions.question: The derivative of logIx is:
questions.explanation: d/dx(ln x) = 1/x. For d/dx(log_a x) = 1/(x lna).

questions.question: The derivative of eI is:
questions.explanation: d/dx(eI) = eI. The exponential function is its own derivative.

questions.question: If y = x² sin x, then dy/dx is (product rule):
questions.explanation: Product rule: (uv)' = u'v+uv'. d/dx(x²·sinx)=2x·sinx+x²·cosx.

questions.question: If y=f(x)/g(x), then dy/dx by quotient rule is:
questions.explanation: Quotient rule: d/dx(f/g)=(f'g–fg')/g². Numerator: derivative of top × bottom – top × derivative of bottom.

questions.question: The derivative of tanI¹x is:
questions.explanation: d/dx(tanI¹x)=1/(1+x²). d/dx(sinI¹x)=1/√(1–x²). d/dx(cosI¹x)=–1/√(1–x²).

questions.question: Second derivative of y=sin x is:
questions.explanation: y'=cosx, y''=d/dx(cosx)=–sinx. Note: y''''=y (4th derivative of sinx = sinx).

questions.question: If x=at², y=2at (parametric), then dy/dx is:
questions.explanation: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt) = 2a/(2at) = 1/t.

questions.question: Rolle's theorem requires: f continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b) and:
questions.explanation: Rolle's theorem: if f(a)=f(b), then ∃c∈(a,b) s.t. f'(c)=0. (Special case of MVT) ∑ ^2PPLIC^2TIONS OF DERIV^2TIVES
questions.hint: 💡 Rolle's theorem: if f(a)=f(b), then ∃c∈(a,b) s.

questions.question: The condition for f(x) to have a local minimum at x=c is:
questions.explanation: f'(c)=0 (critical point). f''(c)>0 → concave up → local min. f''(c)<0 → concave down → local max.

questions.question: The equation of tangent to y=x² at point (2,4) is:
questions.explanation: f'(x)=2x. Slope at (2,4)=4. Tangent: y–4=4(x–2) → y=4x–4.

questions.question: The slope of normal to curve y=x² at (1,1) is:
questions.explanation: f'(1)=2 (slope of tangent). Normal slope = –1/f'(1) = –1/2.

questions.question: ^2 spherical balloon is inflated at 100 cm³/s. Rate of increase of radius when r=5 cm is:
questions.explanation: V=4πr³/3. dV/dt=4πr²(dr/dt). 100=4π(25)(dr/dt) → dr/dt=100/100π=1/π cm/s.

questions.question: The function f(x)=2x³–3x²–12x+4 is increasing in:
The maximum area of rectangle inscribed in a circle of radius r is:
questions.explanation: For rectangle in circle of radius r, if diagonal=2r, max area occurs at square: side=r√2. ^2rea=2r².
questions.hint: 💡 For rectangle in circle of radius r, if diagonal=2r, max area occurs at square: side=r√2.

questions.question: By LMVT, if f(x)=x² on [1,3], the value of c is:
The approximate value of √26 using differentiation (near √25=5) is:
∫xI dx equals (n ≠ –1):
questions.explanation: Power rule for integration: ∫xIdx = xII¹/(n+1)+C (add 1 to power, divide by new power).
questions.hint: 💡 Power rule for integration: ∫xIdx = xII¹/(n+1)+C (add 1 to power, divide by new power).

questions.question: ∫(1/x)dx equals:
questions.explanation: ∫(1/x)dx = ln|x|+C. This is the case n=–1 excluded from power rule.

questions.question: ∫eIdx equals:
questions.explanation: ∫eIdx = e→C. ∫aIdx = aI/lna+C. ∫e^(ax)dx=e^(ax)/a+C.

questions.question: ∫sin x dx equals:
questions.explanation: ∫sinx dx = –cosx+C. ∫cosx dx = sinx+C. (Opposite of differentiating)

questions.question: The value of ∫I^π sin x dx is:
∫x eI dx (by integration by parts) equals:
questions.explanation: ∫xeIdx: u=x, dv=eIdx. =[xeI–∫eIdx]=xeI–e→C=eI(x–1)+C.

questions.question: The area under curve y=x² from x=0 to x=3 is:
questions.explanation: ∫I³x²dx=[x³/3]I³=27/3–0=9 sq units.

questions.question: ∫II¹ x³ dx equals:
questions.explanation: x³ is an odd function. ∫III f(x)dx=0 for odd f. (Directly: [xI/4]II¹=1/4–1/4=0.) ∑ DIFFERENTI^2L EQU^2TIONS

questions.question: Order of differential equation d²y/dx² + 3(dy/dx)³ + y = 0 is:
questions.explanation: Order = order of highest derivative = 2 (d²y/dx²). Degree = power of highest order derivative = 1.

questions.question: Solution of dy/dx = y gives:
questions.explanation: Separate variables: dy/y = dx. Integrate: lny=x+k → y=e^(x+k)=CeI.

questions.question: The integrating factor of dy/dx + Py = Q is:
questions.explanation: For linear ODE dy/dx+Py=Q: IF = e^(∫Pdx). Solution: y×IF = ∫Q×IF dx + C.
questions.hint: 💡 For linear ODE dy/dx+Py=Q: IF = e^(∫Pdx).

questions.question: The degree of (dy/dx)² + y = x is:
questions.explanation: Degree = power of highest order derivative when equation is polynomial in derivatives. Here degree=2. ∑ PROB^2BILITY
questions.hint: 💡 Degree = power of highest order derivative when equation is polynomial in derivatives.

questions.question: Two dice are thrown. Probability that sum is 7 is:
Bayes' theorem: P(^2|B) = P(B|^2)·P(^2)/P(B). This relates to:
questions.explanation: Bayes' theorem is used for conditional probability: updating probability given new evidence. CONGR^2TUL^2TIONS — ^2LL 160 M^2THS QUESTIONS DONE! questions.question: The dimensional formula of angular momentum is:
questions.explanation: L = mvr = [M][LTI¹][L] = [ML²TI¹]. ^2lso L = Iω = [ML²][TI¹] = [ML²TI¹]

questions.question: The percentage error in measurement of radius is 2≠. The percentage error in volume of sphere is:
questions.explanation: V = (4/3)πr³. ≠ error in V = 3 × (≠ error in r) = 3 × 2≠ = 6≠

questions.question: Which of the following pairs has the same dimensional formula
questions.explanation: Torque τ = F×r = [ML²TI²]; Work W = F·d = [ML²TI²]. Same dimensions!
questions.hint: 💡 Torque τ = F×r = [ML²TI²]; Work W = F·d = [ML²TI²].

questions.question: Light year is a unit of:
questions.explanation: 1 light year = distance light travels in one year ≈ 9.46 × 10¹I m. II KINEM^2TICS
questions.hint: 💡 1 light year = distance light travels in one year ≈ 9.

questions.question: ^2 body is thrown vertically upward with velocity u. Time to reach maximum height is:
questions.explanation: ^2t max height, v = 0. Using v = u - gt → 0 = u - gt → t = u/g

questions.question: ^2 projectile is fired at angle θ with horizontal. The horizontal range is maximum when θ equals:
questions.explanation: R = u²sin2θ/g. R is maximum when sin2θ = 1 → 2θ = 90° → θ = 45°

questions.question: Velocity-time graph of uniform acceleration is a:
questions.explanation: v = u + at. This is linear in t → v-t graph is a straight line with slope = a.

questions.question: ^2 ball is dropped from height h. Time to reach ground is:
questions.explanation: h = ½gt² → t = √(2h/g). Initial velocity u = 0 for a dropped body.

questions.question: For a particle moving in a circle of radius r with uniform speed v, centripetal acceleration is:
questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration a = v²/r = ω²r. Directed towards centre. II L^2WS OF MOTION

questions.question: ^2 body of mass 5 kg is acted upon by a net force of 20 N. Its acceleration is:
questions.explanation: Newton's 2nd law: F = ma → a = F/m = 20/5 = 4 m/s²
questions.hint: 💡 Newton's 2nd law: F = ma → a = F/m = 20/5 = 4 m/s²

questions.question: ^2 body of mass 2 kg moving at 10 m/s collides and sticks to a body of mass 3 kg at rest. Their combined velocity is:
questions.explanation: Conservation of momentum: mIuI = (m→mI)v → 2×10 = 5×v → v = 4 m/s
questions.hint: 💡 Conservation of momentum: mIuI = (m→mI)v → 2×10 = 5×v → v = 4 m/s

questions.question: The coefficient of static friction between a block and surface is 0.4 (g=10 m/s²). Maximum static friction on a 5 kg block is:
questions.explanation: f_s(max) = µI × N = µI × mg = 0.4 × 5 × 10 = 20 N

questions.question: Which of the following is a consequence of Newton's Third Law
questions.explanation: momentum Newton's 3rd law (action-reaction) leads directly to conservation of linear momentum.
questions.hint: 💡 momentum Newton's 3rd law (action-reaction) leads directly to conservation of linear momentum.

questions.question: ^2 60 kg person stands in a lift accelerating upward at 2 m/s². ^2pparent weight (g=10 m/s²) is:
questions.explanation: ^2pparent weight = m(g+a) = 60(10+2) = 60×12 = 720 N II WORK, ENERGY & POWER
questions.hint: 💡 ^2pparent weight = m(g+a) = 60(10+2) = 60×12 = 720 N II WORK, ENERGY & POWER

questions.question: The work done by a force F = 5 N on a body through displacement d = 4 m at 60° is:
The kinetic energy of a body of mass 2 kg moving at 6 m/s is:
questions.explanation: KE = ½mv² = ½ × 2 × 6² = ½ × 2 × 36 = 36 J

questions.question: ^2 spring of force constant k is compressed by x. The elastic potential energy stored is:
questions.explanation: Elastic PE = ½kx². This energy is released when spring returns to natural length.

questions.question: ^2 machine does 1000 J of work in 10 s. Its power output is:
In an elastic collision:
options: C) Both momentum and KE are conserved
questions.explanation: and KE are conserved Elastic collision: both linear momentum ^2ND kinetic energy are conserved. Coefficient of restitution = 1. II ROT^2TION^2L MOTION
questions.hint: 💡 and KE are conserved Elastic collision: both linear momentum ^2ND kinetic energy are conserved.

questions.question: The moment of inertia of a solid disc of mass M and radius R about its central axis is:
questions.explanation: I(disc) = ½MR². For ring I = MR², for solid sphere I = 2/5 MR².

questions.question: ^2ngular momentum L = Iω. If I is halved and angular momentum is conserved, ω becomes:
questions.explanation: L = Iω = constant. If I → I/2, then ω → 2ω (like ice skater pulling in arms).

questions.question: Torque τ = r × F. The SI unit of torque is:
questions.explanation: τ = r × F → unit = m × N = N·m. ^2lthough dimensions equal Joule, torque is expressed in N·m.
questions.hint: 💡 τ = r × F → unit = m × N = N·m.

questions.question: ^2 body rolls without slipping. The ratio of rotational KE to total KE for a solid sphere is:
The theorem of parallel axes states that I = Icm + Md². Here d is:
options: B) Distance between the two parallel axes
C) Distance from centre of mass to surface
questions.explanation: the two parallel axes Parallel axis theorem: I = Icm + Md² where d = distance between parallel axis and CM axis. II GR^2VIT^2TION
questions.hint: 💡 the two parallel axes Parallel axis theorem: I = Icm + Md² where d = distance between parallel axis and CM axis.

questions.question: The orbital velocity of a satellite close to Earth's surface (R = 6400 km, g = 9.8 m/s²) is approximately:
Kepler's third law states that T² ∝ r³. If the orbital radius is doubled, the time period becomes:
Escape velocity from Earth's surface is:
questions.explanation: v_esc = √(2GM/R) = √(2gR) ≈ 11.2 km/s for Earth.

questions.question: The gravitational potential energy of a body of mass m at height h above Earth (radius R) is:
questions.explanation: U = -GMm/r where r = R+h from Earth's centre. Negative sign: bound system. II PROPERTIES OF M^2TTER
questions.hint: 💡 U = -GMm/r where r = R+h from Earth's centre.

questions.question: Young's modulus is defined as:
questions.explanation: Longitudinal strain Young's modulus Y = (F/^2)/(∆L/L) = Longitudinal stress / Longitudinal strain.
questions.hint: 💡 Longitudinal strain Young's modulus Y = (F/^2)/(∆L/L) = Longitudinal stress / Longitudinal strain.

questions.question: Surface tension has dimensions:
questions.explanation: T = F/L = [MLTI²]/[L] = [MTI²]. Unit = N/m.

questions.question: Bernoulli's theorem is based on conservation of:
questions.explanation: Bernoulli's equation: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant → conservation of energy per unit volume.
questions.hint: 💡 Bernoulli's equation: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant → conservation of energy per unit volume.

questions.question: The excess pressure inside a soap bubble of radius R and surface tension T is:
questions.explanation: Soap bubble has 2 surfaces. Excess pressure = 4T/R. For liquid drop: 2T/R (1 surface). II THERM^2L PHYSICS

questions.question: For an ideal gas at temperature T, the rms speed of molecules is proportional to:
questions.explanation: v_rms = √(3RT/M) ∝ √T. Doubling T increases v_rms by factor √2.

questions.question: For a monoatomic ideal gas, the ratio γ = Cp/Cv is:
questions.explanation: Monoatomic gas: f=3. Cv = 3R/2, Cp = 5R/2. γ = Cp/Cv = 5/3 ≈ 1.67

questions.question: Newton's law of cooling states that the rate of cooling is proportional to:
options: C) Difference in temperatures of body and surroundings
questions.explanation: temperatures of body and surroundings dQ/dt ∝ (T - TI). Rate of cooling depends on temperature excess over surroundings.
questions.hint: 💡 temperatures of body and surroundings dQ/dt ∝ (T - TI).

questions.question: The efficiency of a Carnot engine operating between 300 K and 500 K is:
Wien's displacement law states that λ_max × T = constant. If T is doubled, λ_max:
questions.explanation: λ_max ∝ 1/T. If T → 2T, then λ_max → λ_max/2. II OSCILL^2TIONS (SHM)

questions.question: The time period of a simple pendulum of length L is:
questions.explanation: T = 2π√(L/g). Time period is independent of mass and amplitude (for small oscillations).

questions.question: For SHM x = ^2 sinωt, the maximum velocity is:
questions.explanation: v = dx/dt = ^2ω cosωt. Maximum when cosωt = 1 → v_max = ^2ω (at mean position).

questions.question: In SHM, at mean position the restoring force is:
questions.explanation: F = -kx. ^2t mean position x = 0 → F = 0. Velocity is maximum here.

questions.question: The time period of a spring-mass system (mass m, spring constant k) is:
questions.explanation: T = 2π√(m/k). Unlike pendulum, it depends on mass but is independent of g and amplitude. II W^2VES & SOUND

questions.question: The speed of sound in air at 0°C is 332 m/s. ^2t 4°C it is approximately:
questions.explanation: v ∝ √T. v = 332√(277/273) ≈ 333.2 m/s. Speed of sound increases with temperature.

questions.question: Doppler effect: ^2 source moves towards a stationary observer. The observed frequency:
questions.explanation: When source approaches observer, wavefronts are compressed → shorter wavelength → higher frequency.
questions.hint: 💡 When source approaches observer, wavefronts are compressed → shorter wavelength → higher frequency.

questions.question: Beats are produced due to:
options: ^2) Interference of two waves of same frequency
B) Superposition of two waves of slightly different
questions.explanation: two waves of slightly different frequencies Beat frequency = |fI - fI|. Beats are periodic variations in loudness due to interference.
questions.hint: 💡 two waves of slightly different frequencies Beat frequency = |fI - fI|.

questions.question: For a standing wave, nodes are points of:
questions.explanation: Nodes: zero displacement (max pressure variation). ^2ntinodes: max displacement (min pressure variation).

questions.question: The frequency of the fundamental mode of vibration of an open pipe of length L is:
questions.explanation: Open pipe: both ends antinodes. Fundamental: L = λ/2 → f = v/2L (closed: v/4L). II ELECTROST^2TICS

questions.question: Coulomb's law: Force between charges qI and qI at distance r is F = kqIqI/r². If distance is tripled, force becomes:
questions.explanation: F ∝ 1/r². If r → 3r, F → F/9. Force decreases by factor 9.

questions.question: Electric potential at distance r from charge q is:
questions.explanation: V = kq/r. Electric field E = kq/r² (note: E ∝ 1/r², V ∝ 1/r).

questions.question: The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor with area ^2, plate separation d and dielectric constant K is:
questions.explanation: C = KεI^2/d. Dielectric constant K increases capacitance by factor K.

questions.question: Energy stored in a capacitor of capacitance C charged to voltage V is:
questions.explanation: U = ½CV² = ½QV = Q²/2C. ^2ll three expressions are equivalent.

questions.question: Gauss's law: For a spherical shell of charge Q, electric field inside is:
questions.explanation: By Gauss's law, E inside a uniformly charged shell = 0 (no enclosed charge for r < R). II CURRENT ELECTRICITY
questions.hint: 💡 By Gauss's law, E inside a uniformly charged shell = 0 (no enclosed charge for r < R).

questions.question: Ohm's law V = IR is valid when:
questions.explanation: constant Ohm's law holds when temperature (and hence resistance) is constant. R changes with T.
questions.hint: 💡 constant Ohm's law holds when temperature (and hence resistance) is constant.

questions.question: Three resistors of 2Ω, 3Ω, 6Ω are connected in parallel. Equivalent resistance is:
Kirchhoff's current law (KCL) is based on:
questions.explanation: charge KCL: ΣI at junction = 0. Charge cannot accumulate at a junction → conservation of charge.

questions.question: The Wheatstone bridge is balanced when:
questions.explanation: Balance condition: P/Q = R/S. Galvanometer shows zero deflection. No current through galvanometer.

questions.question: The power dissipated in a resistor R carrying current I is:
questions.explanation: P = I²R = V²/R = VI. ^2ll are equivalent using Ohm's law V = IR. II M^2GNETIC EFFECTS OF CURRENT

questions.question: The force on a charge q moving with velocity v in magnetic field B is:
questions.explanation: Lorentz force: F = q(v × B) = qvB sinθ. Force is perpendicular to both v and B.
questions.hint: 💡 Lorentz force: F = q(v × B) = qvB sinθ.

questions.question: The magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop of radius R carrying current I is:
questions.explanation: B = µII/2R at centre of circular loop. For solenoid: B = µInI.
questions.hint: 💡 B = µII/2R at centre of circular loop.

questions.question: Moving coil galvanometer works on the principle of:
options: B) Torque on current loop in magnetic field
questions.explanation: loop in magnetic field τ = NI^2B sinθ. Current-carrying coil in B-field experiences torque → deflection proportional to I.
questions.hint: 💡 loop in magnetic field τ = NI^2B sinθ.

questions.question: The SI unit of magnetic flux is:
questions.explanation: Magnetic flux Φ = B·^2. Unit = T·m² = Weber (Wb). 1 Wb = 10I Maxwell (CGS).

questions.question: ^2mpere's circuital law: IB·dl = µIIenc. This is analogous to Gauss's law for:
questions.explanation: Gauss's law: IE·d^2 = q/εI. ^2mpere's law: IB·dl = µII. Both relate field to source quantity. II EMI & ^2LTERN^2TING CURRENT

questions.question: Faraday's law of EMI: The induced EMF is equal to:
options: B) Rate of change of magnetic flux
C) Rate of change of resistance
questions.explanation: magnetic flux e = -dΦ/dt (Faraday's law). Negative sign is Lenz's law (opposing cause).

questions.question: The rms value of ^2C voltage V = VI sinωt is:
questions.explanation: V_rms = VI/√2 ≈ 0.707 VI. For I_rms = II/√2. Mains supply: VI = 325V, V_rms ≈ 230V.

questions.question: ^2t resonance in a series LCR circuit:
questions.explanation: Resonance: XL = XC → ωL = 1/ωC → ωI = 1/√(LC). Impedance Z = R (minimum), current is maximum.

questions.question: Power factor of a purely inductive circuit is:
questions.explanation: PF = cosφ. For pure inductor, φ = 90° (current lags voltage). PF = cos90° = 0. No real power.

questions.question: ^2 transformer steps voltage from 220 V to 11000 V. If primary turns = 200, secondary turns are:
questions.explanation: Vs/Vp = Ns/Np → 11000/220 = Ns/200 → Ns = 50 × 200 = 10000 II R^2Y & W^2VE OPTICS

questions.question: The critical angle for total internal reflection for a medium of refractive index µ = √2 is:
questions.explanation: sin(C) = 1/µ = 1/√2 → C = 45°. TIR occurs when angle of incidence > C.

questions.question: Mirror formula: 1/v + 1/u = 1/f. For a concave mirror of focal length 10 cm with object at 30 cm, image distance is:
In Young's double slit experiment, fringe width β = λD/d. If d is doubled (other things same), β:
questions.explanation: β ∝ 1/d. If d → 2d, then β → β/2. Fringes become narrower.

questions.question: Polarization is a property of:
questions.explanation: Only transverse waves can be polarized. Light (transverse EM wave) can be polarized; sound cannot.
questions.hint: 💡 Only transverse waves can be polarized.

questions.question: Resolving power of a microscope increases when:
options: B) Wavelength of light used decreases
questions.explanation: used decreases RP = 2µsinθ/λ ∝ 1/λ. Smaller λ → better resolution. Electron microscopes use very small λ.

questions.question: In a single slit diffraction pattern, the central maximum is how many times wider than secondary maxima
questions.explanation: Central maximum width = 2λD/a; secondary maxima width = λD/a. Central is twice as wide. II MODERN PHYSICS
questions.hint: 💡 Central maximum width = 2λD/a; secondary maxima width = λD/a.

questions.question: In photoelectric effect, the stopping potential VI is related to max KE of photoelectrons by:
questions.explanation: Stopping potential VI just stops fastest electrons. KE_max = eVI (energy of electron retarded by VI).
questions.hint: 💡 Stopping potential VI just stops fastest electrons.

questions.question: Bohr's model: Radius of nth orbit of hydrogen is:
questions.explanation: rI = n²aI where aI = 0.529 Å (Bohr radius). rI = 0.529 Å, rI = 4 × 0.529 Å.

questions.question: In radioactive decay, the half-life T½ and decay constant λ are related by:
questions.explanation: N = NIe^(-λt). ^2t t = T½: N = NI/2 → e^(-λT½) = 1/2 → T½ = ln2/λ = 0.693/λ

questions.question: In nuclear fission, the energy released is due to:
options: ^2) Increase in binding energy per nucleon
B) Decrease in binding energy per nucleon
C) Conversion of electron to positron
questions.explanation: energy per nucleon Heavy nuclei (low BE/nucleon) split into medium nuclei (high BE/nucleon). Difference → energy (E = ∆mc²).
questions.hint: 💡 energy per nucleon Heavy nuclei (low BE/nucleon) split into medium nuclei (high BE/nucleon).

questions.question: In α-decay, the mass number ^2 and atomic number Z change as:
questions.explanation: α particle = IIHe. In α-decay: ^2 → ^2-4, Z → Z-2. E.g., ²³IU → ²³ITh + IHe II SEMICONDUCTOR DEVICES

questions.question: In a p-n junction diode under forward bias:
options: B) Width of depletion layer decreases
questions.explanation: layer decreases Forward bias opposes built-in potential → depletion layer narrows → current flows easily.
questions.hint: 💡 layer decreases Forward bias opposes built-in potential → depletion layer narrows → current flows easily.

questions.question: ^2 Zener diode is used as:
questions.explanation: Zener diode maintains constant voltage across load despite variations in input voltage or load current.
questions.hint: 💡 Zener diode maintains constant voltage across load despite variations in input voltage or load current.

questions.question: In a common emitter (CE) transistor amplifier, the voltage gain depends on:
options: B) β and ratio of collector to base resistance
questions.explanation: collector to base resistance Voltage gain ^2v = β × (Rc/Rb) in CE configuration. β = Ic/Ib (current gain). YOU'VE COMPLETED ^2LL 80 PHYSICS QUESTIONS!
